landmarks.landmark_blurb: Lived here as a boy, per the 1850 census

narrative_blocks.header: He was Ossian’s first Republican supervisor
narrative_blocks.body: William Mansfield White, son of Congressman Hugh White, farmed at Canaseraga and became the first Republican supervisor of the town of Ossian. He also served as an Episcopal lay reader and church warden.

narrative_blocks.header: Three terms in Congress for the Whigs
narrative_blocks.body: A Whig elected to three Congresses from New York's sixteenth district between 1845 and 1851, Hugh White chaired the House Committee on Agriculture. He had trained in law but never practiced, turning instead to business.

narrative_blocks.header: He made America's first waterproof cement
narrative_blocks.body: With his brother Canvass White, the Erie Canal engineer who patented American hydraulic cement, Hugh ran the Rosendale Cement Works, whose product was the first water-proof cement made in the country and was used on the Croton Aqueduct. He also built mills, ran a bank, and helped build the Michigan Southern and Northern Indiana Railroad.

narrative_blocks.header: Townsfolk saved his mansion from the wreckers
narrative_blocks.body: Hugh White's 1830 Waterford homestead, built of brick and timber shipped down the Erie Canal, was marked for demolition in 1964. Within weeks a group of residents raised the money to buy it and moved it to a new site, where it became the Waterford Historical Museum.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built and lived in this 1830 Waterford homestead
Settled here after her 1828 marriage to Hugh White

bio.photo_notes: Maria Mills Mansfield White (1808–1888), wife of Hugh White.

sources.short: Moffat, R. Burnham. Pierrepont Genealogies from Norman times to 1913 (1913)
Brooklyn Historical Society: Pierrepont Family Papers ARC.263

notable.notable_blurb: Pierrepont Manor namesake; Jefferson County railroad president

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built the three-story 26-room Manor House in 1826 on 7.17 acres; the hamlet itself was named for him.

narrative_blocks.header: Met every train wearing cape with small dog
narrative_blocks.body: From May 28, 1851 — the day the first railroad train steamed into the hamlet of Pierrepont Manor — almost to the day of his death December 20, 1885, William Constable Pierrepont met every train that passed through that community, always with watch in hand to check the scheduled time. He was a little man wearing a long cape and followed by a small dog. The thirty-four-year ritual of standing trackside at every train arrival was the defining public habit of his second half of life.

From about 1820 — when he was seventeen — William Constable Pierrepont moved to Bear Creek New York to manage his family's roughly one-million-acre landholdings in Jefferson, Lewis, and Saint Lawrence Counties of upstate New York. The town of Bear Creek was renamed Pierrepont Manor in his honor. He built both the Pierrepont Manor house and the Land Office — buildings now on the National Register of Historic Places.

narrative_blocks.header: President Watertown & Rome Railroad first into Jefferson
narrative_blocks.body: On April 10, 1851, William Constable Pierrepont was elected president of the Watertown & Rome Railroad — the elections precisely timed with the railroad's arrival at Pierrepont Manor on May 28, 1851. That arrival was the first railroad train into Jefferson County New York. He simultaneously founded and endowed Zion Episcopal Church at Pierrepont Manor in 1835 — the small village's denominational anchor for the rest of the century.

narrative_blocks.header: Built 26-room Manor house and Zion Episcopal Church
narrative_blocks.body: In 1826 William Constable Pierrepont built his three-story 26-room frame Manor House on seven and a sevenths acres — an impressive structure with tall pillars and a colonial doorway with leaded glass transome and sidelights, sitting back from the road in a picturesque grove. Ten years later in 1836 he built Zion Episcopal Church across the street from the Manor house and Land Office; the church served the community he had named and given form to.

narrative_blocks.header: Four-term Town supervisor; one-term State Assemblyman
narrative_blocks.body: William Constable Pierrepont served as Town of Ellisburg supervisor in 1840, 1843, 1846, and 1847 — four non-consecutive terms across the decade — and as New York State Assemblyman in 1840-1841. The civic offices ran in parallel with his ongoing land-management business, his railroad presidency from 1851, and his Zion Episcopal Church patronage. His personal wealth at the time was estimated at five hundred thousand dollars.

narrative_blocks.header: Corresponded with Astronomer Royal about Great Pyramid
narrative_blocks.body: William Constable Pierrepont's intellectual interests ranged far beyond land management. He corresponded with the Astronomer Royal Professor Charles Piazzi Smyth about Smyth's controversial calculations on the Great Pyramid of Giza — the mid-Victorian astronomical-archaeological project that attempted to derive sacred-measurement units from pyramid geometry. His own maps at age seventy-six were described by contemporaries as 'marvels of accuracy.' He kept sixty-two years of daily-weather and land-sale diaries from 1823 through 1885 — the archive now at Cornell University Library, Collection 1094.

narrative_blocks.header: Eldest of Hezekiah Pierrepont; five daughters, two sons
narrative_blocks.body: William Constable Pierrepont was born October 3, 1803 in New York City, eldest son of Hezekiah Beers Pierrepont and Anna Maria Constable — making him a seventh-generation descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker. He had seven children, five daughters and two sons (both sons died young), and died December 20, 1885 at Pierrepont Manor at age eighty-two, buried in Pierrepont Manor Cemetery. The Constable-Pierrepont Papers covering 1762-1911 are at Columbia University Library.
